learning_outcomes.description: appreciate ethical, social and political issues raised by human relationships to the environment
understand how ethical and political theory can help frame arguments relating to environmental obligations or policies.
appreciate the diverse disciplinary perspectives which deal with environmental problems
better and more precisely analyse and articulate problems
better analyse and evaluate complex ethical dilemmas
think critically, systemically and carefully in analysing complex problems
improved capacity to develop depth and reasoning in arguments relating to environmental imperatives.

assessments.task_description:
You are required to attend tutorials across the semester (Weeks 2-9, 11-12), via a live, online, zoom link with your cameras on. Students are assessed on their engagement with discussion and activities in tutorials. Marks will be awarded for evidence of having listened to the lectures and completed the required readings for each tutorial, for engaging substantially in class discussions, and for respectfully fostering group discussions. Attendance via zoom is necessary to fulfil these participation tasks.

Please see participation assessment criteria below under "Additional Course Grading information".
assessments.submission_guidelines: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
This task is Part 1 of an essay task that has both a written component and viva voce (live voice) component.

Essay, Written Component: 2000 word philosophy essay arguing for a position (+/- 10% word limit) on a case-study provided. The word limit does NOT include titles, citations, references and bibliographic information. Please include a word count excluding these items at the beginning of your submission. Direct quotation and discursive footnotes (i.e. further textual explanations included in a footnote) ARE included in the word count.

You may use APA7 or Chicago 18th reference styles, but you must use the same consistently throughout, and whichever version you include must include page numbers (i.e. page number version of your chosen style) for paginated sources or section headings for unpaginated sources, where points you are relying on occur. Any ideas arising from the use of AI must be referenced according to UQ Policy and the Library Reference guide, as you would any other source. In other words, you may not claim use of AI as your own work.

This written component has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. Use of AI needs to be appropriately referenced.

Topics and further advice about completion of this assignment will be available in the "Assessments" folder on Blackboard.

Requirements for Submission of Essays:

  1. You must submit an electronic copy only through Turnitin under 'Assessment' on Blackboard. Check your paper has submitted properly; you should receive and keep a confirmation.
  2. Please include your name and topic number in the naming of your document.
  3. Include a word count of your paper excluding titles, citation, reference and/ or bibliographic information.
  4. Students are responsible for familiarizing themselves with School policy regarding academic advice, assessment, plagiarism, etc. Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy (http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct and refer to Section 6.1 of this Electronic Course Profile. PLAGIARISM is an academic offence and will be penalized.


Grading Criteria for your argumentative essay, written component:

  1. Ability to define the topic or philosophical issue under debate. (Topic definition.)
  2. Ability to construct a well-reasoned argument for a certain point of view. (Argument and Structure.)
  3. Depth and breadth of understanding of the various positions in a philosophical debate. (Scope and Depth of Treatment)
  4. Insight and/or originality in interpreting texts or constructing a point of view or argument. (Originality)
  5. Capacity to produce a polished, well-written and appropriately referenced essay. (Presentation)


See assessment criteria below under "Additional Course Grading information" for more detailed description of criteria for the essay, written component.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date: These will be scheduled during the 2-3 weeks following the written submission.
assessments.task_description:
This task is Part 2 of an essay task that has both a written component and viva voce (live voice) component.

The Viva Voce involves an oral defence of the student's written essay submission (Part 1 above). Students will be interviewed online, with cameras being mandatory, by the marker in small groups in which they briefly describe their case study and the position they defended in their essay to the group, followed by a small number of bespoke questions relating to the student's own written essay submission. Students may view a copy of their written submission during the viva voce.

The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

Further details and guidance for the Viva Voce component of the Essay will be available in the Assessments folder on Blackboard.

The Viva Voce component of the Essay task will be marked according to the following criteria:

  1. Understanding of the thought processes and logical relationships of ideas in the written submission.
  2. Ability to clarify claims in essay upon further questioning.
  3. Familiarity and understanding of sources mentioned or referenced in the written submission.
  4. Familiarity and understanding of course materials relevant to essay.
  5. Capacity to engage with and defend claims under questioning.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Students will complete an in-person exam during the final exam block, with a mixture of multiple-choice, short answer, and essay questions.

External students will need to make arrangements to sit this exam at the UQ St Lucia campus or a testing centre approved by UQ. Students within 200km of St Lucia will need to attend UQ St Lucia campus to sit the exam. Students whose address is more than 200 km from the St Lucia campus will need to sit the exam at the nearest UQ designated exam centre.

Information regarding designated UQ exam centres is here:

Off-campus exams - my.UQ - University of Queensland

Further advice as to structure and preparation for this exam will be provided in the "Assessments" folder on Blackboard.

Please see assessment criteria below under "Additional Course Grading information".
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.
